stem: The number of calls arriving at a switchboard in one hour follows a Poisson distribution with mean \( \lambda = [[lam]] \). What is the probability that exactly [[k]] calls arrive in a given hour?
solution: For a Poisson variable with mean \( \lambda = [[lam]] \), \( P(X = [[k]]) = \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^{[[k]]}}{[[k]]!} = \frac{e^{-[[lam]]}\,[[lam]]^{[[k]]}}{[[k]]!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the \( \frac{1}{[[k]]!} \) normalizing factor.
This omits the \( e^{-\lambda} \) factor.
This is \( P(X = [[k]] + 1) \), shifted up by one event.
This is \( P(X = [[k]] - 1) \), shifted down by one event.
This divides by \( ([[k]] + 1)! \) instead of \( [[k]]! \).
This is the complement \( 1 - P(X = [[k]]) \).

stem: The number of typos on a page follows a Poisson distribution with mean \( \lambda = [[lam]] \). What is the probability that a given page has AT MOST [[k]] typos (that is, \( P(X \le [[k]]) \))?
solution: The CDF sums the pmf over all counts up to [[k]]: \( P(X \le [[k]]) = \sum_{i=0}^{[[k]]} \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^i}{i!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is only the single term \( P(X = [[k]]) \), not the cumulative sum up to it.
This is the complement of \( P(X=0) \), i.e. \( P(X \ge 1) \), regardless of [[k]].
This uses the wrong number of terms (fixed at two), not matching the requested cutoff [[k]].
This is \( P(X = [[k]]+1) \), a single shifted term, not the cumulative sum.
This is the complement \( P(X > [[k]]) \) rather than \( P(X \le [[k]]) \).
This linearly scales \( e^{-\lambda} \) by [[k]] instead of summing the pmf terms.

stem: The number of flaws on a sheet of material follows a Poisson distribution with mean \( \lambda = \frac{[[lamn]]}{[[lamd]]} \). What is the probability that a given sheet has AT LEAST one flaw?
distractor_rationales: This is \( P(X = 0) \) itself, not its complement.
This treats \( \lambda \) as if it were already a probability, ignoring the Poisson structure entirely.
This is \( 1 - P(X \le 1) \), i.e. \( P(X \ge 2) \), one flaw too many excluded.
This is \( P(X = 1) \), a single pmf term, not the complement of \( P(X=0) \).
This incorrectly complements \( \lambda \) directly instead of \( e^{-\lambda} \).
This uses \( e^{\lambda} \) with the wrong sign in the exponent.

stem: The number of accidents at an intersection in a month follows a Poisson distribution with mean \( \lambda = \frac{[[lamn]]}{[[lamd]]} \). What is the probability of AT LEAST two accidents in a given month?
distractor_rationales: This only excludes \( P(X=0) \), giving \( P(X \ge 1) \) instead of \( P(X \ge 2) \).
This is \( P(X \le 1) \) itself, not its complement.
This is just the \( P(X=2) \) pmf term, not the tail probability.
This excludes only part of the \( P(X=1) \) term, forgetting the constant piece.
This is \( P(X=0) \) alone.
This omits the \( e^{-\lambda} \) factor and the complement entirely.

stem: For a Poisson random variable \( X \) with mean \( \lambda = [[lam]] \), what is the ratio \( \dfrac{P(X = [[k]] + 1)}{P(X = [[k]])} \)?
solution: The Poisson pmf satisfies \( \frac{P(X=k+1)}{P(X=k)} = \frac{\lambda}{k+1} \), so with \( k = [[k]] \) and \( \lambda = [[lam]] \), the ratio is \( \frac{[[lam]]}{[[k]] + 1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( k \) instead of \( k+1 \) in the denominator.
This inverts the true ratio.
This uses \( k-1 \) instead of \( k+1 \) in the denominator.
This multiplies instead of dividing.
This is off by one, using \( k+2 \) instead of \( k+1 \).
This has the numerator and denominator's roles swapped.

stem: A Poisson random variable \( X \) with mean \( \lambda \) satisfies \( P(X = [[j]]) = P(X = [[j]] + 1) \). What is the value of \( \lambda \)?

answer_spec.equation: exp(-x) * x ** [[j]] / factorial([[j]]) - exp(-x) * x ** ([[j]] + 1) / factorial([[j]] + 1) = 0

solution: Dividing the equal-probability condition through, \( 1 = \frac{\lambda}{[[j]]+1} \), so \( \lambda = [[j]] + 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( j \) itself instead of \( j+1 \).
This is off by one below the correct value.
This is off by one above the correct value (using \( j+2 \)).
This inverts the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
